name: International Alba White Truffle Fair (Fiera Internazionale del Tartufo Bianco d'Alba)
tagline: Eight foggy autumn weekends when a small Piedmont town becomes the perfumed capital of the gastronomic world.
dates: 10 October – 6 December 2026 (96th edition, confirmed; World Truffle Market open Saturdays, Sundays and holidays)
history: The fair is the invention of one man: Giacomo Morra, the hotelier of Alba's Hotel Savona, who staged the town's first truffle exhibition in 1928–29 to lure attention to the Langhe's buried autumn treasure, Tuber magnatum Pico — the white truffle that grows wild in these hills and nowhere better. The 'exhibition sample fair of the renowned Langhe truffles' was timed for late autumn, when the fungus reaches full maturity, and was an immediate success. By 1933 it had formally become the Truffle Fair, and that same year the New York Times crowned Morra the 'King of Truffles.'

Morra was also a marketing genius decades ahead of his time. From 1949 he began dispatching the finest truffle of each season to the world's most famous people — Rita Hayworth, Harry Truman, Winston Churchill, Marilyn Monroe, Alfred Hitchcock, Dwight Eisenhower — turning an obscure Piedmontese fungus into a global object of desire and effectively inventing Italian food-and-wine tourism. His company, Tartufi Morra (founded 1930), was the first to commercialize and preserve the Alba white truffle, and the celebrity-truffle tradition he started still echoes in today's World White Truffle Auction at the castle of Grinzane Cavour, where single specimens have fetched six-figure sums for charity.

Nearly a century on, the fair anchors the identity of an entire region. Italian truffle hunting ('cerca e cavatura del tartufo') was inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2021, Alba is a UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy, and the surrounding Langhe vineyards of Barolo and Barbaresco are themselves a World Heritage landscape. Around the weekend World Market in the Cortile della Maddalena swirl cooking shows with star chefs, sensory analysis workshops, the medieval donkey Palio and costumed parades — a folk festival and a haute-cuisine summit happening in the same fog-wrapped streets.
whyGo: Nowhere else can you smell, judge and buy the world's finest white truffles directly from the hunters who dug them hours earlier, then eat them shaved over hand-cut tajarin in the town where the cult began. The fair coincides with the Barolo and Barbaresco harvest afterglow, so the greatest truffle event on earth doubles as a wine pilgrimage. For a Zürich-based traveler it is arguably the best-value world-class food event within a half-day's reach.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EC direct via Gotthard, ~3h20) → Torino Porta Susa (Frecciarossa, ~1h) → Alba (regional SFM4 line, ~1h15)
gettingThere.cost: CHF 60–140 return Zürich–Milan (supersaver) + €25–45 for the Italian legs
gettingThere.notes: Book SBB supersaver and Trenitalia advance fares ~2-3 months out; the reopened Turin–Alba rail link avoids the old bus transfer via Asti

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Milan Malpensa (Swiss, ~55 min), then rental car ~2h to Alba, or Malpensa Express + train via Turin
gettingThere.duration: 4h30–5h door to door with a car
gettingThere.notes: A car is near-essential for the Barolo/Barbaresco villages anyway; check for seasonal Zürich→Turin (TRN) flights, which put you 75 min from Alba

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Gotthard tunnel → Milan tangenziale → A21/A33 to Alba (~460 km)
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 40 Swiss vignette + €25–35 Italian tolls + fuel (roughly €120–160 return)
gettingThere.notes: Best option for a weekend with wine purchases in the boot; November fog on the Po plain can slow the final stretch — allow buffer

costs.notes: Full-trip estimate from Zürich with 2-3 nights. Fair entry is cheap (€5-6 online); it's the truffle itself that drives cost — roughly €4-5 per gram in a normal season, so a modest 30 g specimen runs €120-150 and a truffle-shaving supplement at restaurants €30-50. Alba hotels surge hard on fair weekends; sleeping in Bra, Asti or a wine village halves lodging costs. Splurge tier assumes a vineyard relais, the Piazza Duomo tasting menu (€350+) and a private truffle hunt.

booking.label: Langhe tourism portal (area info, truffle hunts)

weather: Expect classic Piedmont autumn: 5–15°C in October sliding to 0–8°C by late November, with frequent morning fog (the 'nebbia' that names Nebbiolo) and a fair chance of rain. Pack layers, a waterproof shell, and sturdy shoes — cobblestones in town, real mud if you join a truffle hunt in the woods.
tips: The World Market runs weekends only — arrive Saturday at opening (09:00-ish) before the coach tours; the aroma in the hall is at its headiest early.
Mid-October truffles are fine, but locals say the peak of aroma comes after the first cold snaps in November — and prices sometimes soften late in the season.

Buying a truffle: smell it (intense, garlicky-honeyed, never ammonia), check firmness, and use the fair's on-site quality-control judges who verify weight and grade — that certification is the whole point of buying here rather than from a street hawker.
Book restaurants and truffle hunts 4-6 weeks ahead for fair weekends; Piazza Duomo and the popular hunts sell out months out.
Bring some cash for the market and be ready to negotiate politely — prices are per gram and move daily like a commodity exchange.
Stay Sunday–Tuesday and use the quiet weekdays for Barolo, Barbaresco and the WiMu wine museum: you get the fair's buzz and the empty vineyards.
A whole white truffle survives about a week wrapped in paper towel in a sealed jar in the fridge — perfectly feasible to carry home to Zürich (EU→CH personal food allowances apply; declare if asked).

localRecs.name: Piazza Duomo
localRecs.note: Enrico Crippa's three-Michelin-star temple on the main square — the definitive high-wire treatment of Langhe produce; book months ahead for truffle season

localRecs.name: La Piola
localRecs.note: Piazza Duomo's easygoing ground-floor sibling — tajarin al tartufo and vitello tonnato at sane prices, same obsessive sourcing

localRecs.name: Osteria dell'Arco
localRecs.note: Slow Food stalwart near the market — honest Langhe cooking and a deep, fairly priced Barolo list

localRecs.name: Castello di Grinzane Cavour
localRecs.note: UNESCO-listed castle 15 min from Alba — regional enoteca, truffle-auction venue, and sweeping vineyard views

localRecs.name: Via Vittorio Emanuele (Via Maestra)
localRecs.note: Alba's medieval spine — hazelnut and torrone shops, truffle dealers, and the hundred-tower skyline; Ferrero's Nutella empire was born here

photos.caption: Dealers' stalls at Alba's white truffle market, where specimens are weighed and traded like gems
